query: effectiveness of daprodustat for treatment of anemia

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Iron Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: HIF – prolyl hydroxylase inhibitors (HIF – PHIs) are a novel class of therapeutic agents that raise erythropoietin levels by stabilizing HIF levels, thereby increasing endogenous erythropoietin production. HIF–PHIs also decrease hepcidin levels. In 2023, daprodustat was approved by the FDA for use in patients on dialysis for longer than 4 months. This compound is currently not approved for use in non-dialysis patients. [62] [63] [62]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Newer and potentially less expensive ESA alternatives include epoetin alfa-epbx, a genetically engineered recombinant human erythropoietin, and CERA. HIF – PHIs are a novel class of therapeutic agents that raise erythropoietin levels by stabilizing HIF, which in turn boosts endogenous erythropoietin production. Currently, daprodustat is the only FDA-approved medication in this class. Several other new agents are available, including ferric pyrophosphate (delivered through dialysate), ferric citrate (which also acts as an oral phosphate binder), ziltivekimab (an anti-inflammatory antibody), and ferric maltol/sucrosomial iron (which bypasses stomach absorption and minimizes gastrointestinal effects). Blood transfusions should be avoided as much as possible, especially for patients who may be renal transplant candidates, to prevent allosensitization.

retrieved_docs.title: Erythropoietin Stimulating Agents -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Hypoxia-inducible factor prolyl hydroxylase inhibitors (HIF-PHIs): Due to synergism, concurrent administration of ESA with HIF-PHIs, such as roxadustat and vadadustat, is not advisable.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Iron Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA has rejected several other drugs in this class, which are still currently used in other countries. This class of medication can be taken orally rather than IV or SC. Preliminary evidence comparing ESAs to HIF–PHIs suggests the possibility of increased cardiovascular events in non-dialysis-dependent CKD patients (but not dialysis-dependent patients). [48] These medications are thought to have similar adverse effects to ESAs, including the risk of perpetuating malignant cells. [59] [64] In addition, HIF is thought to contribute to angiogenesis, which could worsen conditions such as retinopathy. The HIF activation pathways also contribute to cyst formation in polycystic kidney disease, and effects on cyst growth are also unknown. So far, data has not shown an increased risk above ESAs, but given the relative newness of this class of medications, the full effects may not yet be known. [2] [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Treatment / Management -- Iron Overload
retrieved_docs.content: When do patients need modification of treatment? Treatment needs to be intensified if LIC > 15 mg Fe/gm dry weight and deescalated when LIC < 3 mg Fe/gm dry weight. Treatment needs to be intensified if serum ferritin > 2500 IU/L and deescalated when serum ferritin < 300 IU/L Treatment needs to be intensified when cardiac MRI shows T2* < 15 milliseconds or when cardiac symptoms occur (like heart failure, arrhythmias) Iron Chelators

retrieved_docs.title: Sideroblastic Anemia -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Emerging treatments include luspatercept, an agent that stimulates late-stage erythropoiesis. [35] In the randomized, double-blind MEDALIST trial, luspatercept significantly reduced the need for red blood cell transfusions and improved hematologic outcomes in transfusion-dependent patients with low-risk MDS with ring sideroblasts. [36] Luspatercept can be used as a first-line treatment or after trying an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent.

retrieved_docs.title: Aplastic Anemia -- Treatment / Management -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should monitor for secondary hemochromatosis and administer iron chelation therapy when clinically necessary. The use of growth factors, eg, erythropoietin or granulocyte colony-stimulating factors, is not recommended due to a lack of precursor cells required to achieve sufficient physiological responses.

retrieved_docs.title: Mean Corpuscular Volume -- Treatment / Management -- Normocytic Anemia
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for patients with paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria typically involves the use of eculizumab, which can effectively reduce hemolysis and prevent complications such as portal hypertension resulting from venous thromboses. [29] Blood transfusions may be necessary in severe anemia to restore hemoglobin levels. Patients with bone marrow failure may undergo hematopoietic stem cell transplantation as a potential treatment option. For patients with macroangiopathic hemolytic anemia secondary to conditions such as prosthetic heart valves or aortic stenosis, surgical interventions such as valve repair or replacement may be considered. Patients with intravascular hemolysis due to malaria or babesiosis require treatment with appropriate anti-malarial or babesiosis medications to manage the underlying infection effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Iron Overload -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Several novel concepts that may help effectively treat iron overload and the patient's overall survival are under investigation. A randomized controlled trial on amlodipine, a calcium channel blocker as an adjuvant iron chelator, shows a significant decrease in myocardial iron concentration. [34] Several phase 2 studies have shown improved adherence with a film-coated oral tablet of deferasirox compared to a dispersible tablet. [35] Gene therapy is another promising option to reduce the transfusion requirement by improving endogenous erythropoiesis. [36] A janus kinase (JAK2) inhibitor and hepcidin analog are additional investigational therapies. [37] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Erythropoiesis-Stimulating Agents
retrieved_docs.content: The erythropoietin analogs epoetin alfa and darbepoetin alfa are the 2 ESAs generally used in managing anemia of CKD. Produced by recombinant DNA technology through cell cultures, they have similar efficacy and adverse effect profiles, except for the longer half-life of darbepoetin alfa, which allows for less frequent dosing. [36] [37] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Erythropoietin Stimulating Agents -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: ASCO suggests that ESAs may also be considered for patients with lower-risk myelodysplastic syndromes and a serum EPO level ≤500 IU/L. [4] A systematic review and network meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials assessed therapeutic strategies for reducing red blood cell (RBC) transfusion in ICU patients. Combination therapy with iron and ESA reduced transfusion requirement compared to placebo/no treatment (RR, 0.60 [95% CI, 0.49-0.74]). Iron and EPOO monotherapies also demonstrated potential benefits. However, given the risks associated with ESA therapy, this indication warrants further investigation. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Iron Therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric pyrophosphate is a novel water-soluble, carbohydrate-free, complex iron salt administered via the dialysate during hemodialysis and was FDA-approved in 2015. This compound is designed to be added to the bicarbonate concentrate of every dialysis treatment, delivering about 7 mg of iron per treatment. Donating iron directly to transferrin may help avoid iron sequestration in reticuloendothelial macrophages. The CRUISE 1 and 2 trials showed that ferric pyrophosphate significantly increases iron indices compared to placebo without significant adverse events. [60] [61] Hypoxia-Inducible Factor–Prolyl Hydroxylase Inhibitors

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Erythropoiesis-Stimulating Agents
retrieved_docs.content: In all patients with CKD, regardless of the need for dialysis, the goal hemoglobin using ESAs is less than 11.5 g/dL. Multiple trials, including CHOIR, NHCT, and TREAT, have assessed the superiority of targeting hemoglobin to 'normal' versus lower ranges. These trials demonstrated higher mortality, thrombosis, and adverse cerebrovascular and cardiovascular events with higher levels of ESAs. The FDA has also issued a warning regarding the increased risk of death, severe adverse cardiovascular events, and stroke when ESAs are administered to target Hb levels above 11 g/dL. [44] [45] [46] [47] [48] This appears to be the higher ESA dose rather than the resulting higher hemoglobin levels, causing the adverse effects, possibly related to ESAs' effects on vascular remodeling and vasoconstriction. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Luspatercept -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Luspatercept is also indicated for patients who fail ESA therapy and fall into one of the following scenarios: 1) require 2 or more red blood cell units over 8 weeks with very low-to-intermediate risk myelodysplastic syndromes with ring sideroblasts (MDS-RS), or 2) have a myelodysplastic/myeloproliferative neoplasm with ring sideroblasts and thrombocytosis (MDS/MPN-RS-T). The ringed sideroblastic phenotype subgroup of patients typically demonstrates a higher response rate. [5] Refractory anemia with ring sideroblasts (RARS) and iron overload are complications managed similarly to low-risk MDS and MPN. Patients with MDS and an SF3B1 mutation demonstrate a homogeneous disease phenotype characterized by dysplasia of granulocytic or megakaryocytic lineages, suggesting that the SF3B1 region significantly affects the severity or subtype of MDS. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Erythropoiesis-Stimulating Agents
retrieved_docs.content: As per Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes (KDIGO) guidelines, ESAs are typically considered in patients with CKD when hemoglobin levels drop below 10 g/dL. However, ESA treatment is individualized based on factors such as anemia symptoms, transfusion requirements, the rate of hemoglobin decline, and response to iron therapy. Erythropoietin (50-100 units/kg IV or subcutaneously [SC]) is usually administered every 1 to 2 weeks, while darbepoetin alfa is dosed every 2 to 4 weeks. For dialysis patients, erythropoietin is given with each dialysis session (3 times a week), whereas darbepoetin alfa is administered once weekly.

retrieved_docs.title: Anemia of Chronic Kidney Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Ziltivekimab
retrieved_docs.content: Ziltivekimab is a human immunoglobulin G (IgG) monoclonal antibody targeting interleukin (IL)-6 – an inflammatory cytokine. In patients with CKD stages 3 to 5, Ziltivekimab has demonstrated the ability to reduce inflammation, increase albumin and hemoglobin levels, and improve iron indices compared to placebo. IL-6 is associated with increased hepcidin expression, which may explain the therapeutic benefits of Ziltivekimab. [1] [65] [66]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2039
retrieved_docs.content: Iron in other combinations is classified as an iron antianemic preparation. Iron antianemic preparations are therapeutic agents used to replenish iron stores and treat iron‑deficiency anemia. Formulations described as “iron in other combinations” contain iron together with additional compounds or nutrients, enhancing absorption or providing complementary benefits while still serving the primary purpose of correcting iron deficiency. Recognizing these combination products as a subset of iron antianemic preparations helps clinicians select appropriate therapy for patients with anemia, especially when co‑existing deficiencies or specific tolerability issues are present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Darbepoetin alfa is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as a member of the “other antianemic preparations” group, indicating its role as an erythropoiesis‑stimulating agent used to treat various forms of anemia. This classification situates darbepoetin alfa among drugs that are specifically designed to increase red blood cell production, distinguishing it from iron supplements or other traditional antianemic therapies. Recognizing darbepoetin alfa’s placement in the ATC category of other antianemic preparations helps clinicians understand its therapeutic purpose, mechanism of action, and appropriate use in managing anemia‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2793
retrieved_docs.content: Iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations represent a therapeutic class of iron‑based agents used to treat iron‑deficiency anemia, and this class includes specific compounds such as ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate. Both ferric hydroxide and sodium ironedetate are members of the iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations group, providing clinicians with oral options to replenish deficient iron stores in patients. Understanding that these individual agents belong to the same antianemic class helps guide appropriate selection and dosing in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1514
retrieved_docs.content: Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations are classified as members of the askorbát železnatý (iron‑ascorbate) group, indicating that these oral anemia treatments contain bivalent iron complexed with ascorbic acid.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1912
retrieved_docs.content: Ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate are both members of the therapeutic class identified as iron trivalent, oral antianemic preparations. This class comprises oral formulations that deliver trivalent iron to patients with iron‑deficiency anemia, thereby replenishing depleted iron stores and supporting hemoglobin synthesis. By belonging to the same category, ferric maltol and sodium ironedetate share a common mechanism of action and are used interchangeably as oral options for correcting iron deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 31
retrieved_docs.content: erythroid precursors; when combined with iron—the essential mineral for hemoglobin synthesis—it corrects the hematologic abnormalities of macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia. Products in the “Iron in other combinations” group, such as **FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITINS** (ascorbic acid 40 mg, ferrous fumarate 62.5 mg, folic acid 1 mg, niacin 3 mg, polysaccharide iron complex 62.5 mg), exemplify this therapeutic strategy. Additional iron‑containing agents include iron ascorbate (askorbát železnatý), and broader antianemic options are represented by **darbepeotin alfa**, classified under “other antianemic preparations” in the ATC system. Vitamins are further organized by subclass relationships: **vitamin A (plain)** belongs to the vitamin A‑and‑D family and may be combined with vitamin D; **vitamin B1 (plain)** may appear alone or with vitamin B6 and vitamin B12; **vitamin B12** includes cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, all members of the vitamin B12 family. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1270
retrieved_docs.content: Iron bivalent, oral antianemic preparations are a specific type of iron antianemic preparations and are classified as members of the askorbát železnatý (iron‑ascorbate) group. As oral formulations that provide bivalent iron, they are used to treat iron‑deficiency anemia, aligning them with the broader class of iron antianemic agents while being distinguished by their inclusion in the iron‑ascorbate category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2129
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified dialysis (غسيل الكلى غير محدد) frequently co‑occurs with anemia (فقر الدم), indicating that patients receiving dialysis are commonly affected by low hemoglobin levels. Acute posthemorrhagic anemia is classified as a specific type of anemia, making it a subcategory of فقر الدم. Consequently, the presence of dialysis‑related anemia underscores the need to monitor and treat various forms of anemia, including acute posthemorrhagic anemia, in patients undergoing dialysis to improve clinical outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1391
retrieved_docs.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an “Other deficiency anemia,” placing it within a broader category of deficiency‑related blood disorders. The “Other deficiency anemias” group includes folate‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات), iron‑deficiency anemia (فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات), and a non‑regenerative form of anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) as a subcategory. It also encompasses hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmune mechanisms (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية). By situating anemia in chronic kidney disease among these specific deficiency and hemolytic conditions, clinicians can appreciate the diverse etiologic pathways—nutrient deficiencies and non‑autoimmune hemolysis—that may contribute to anemia in this patient population, guiding more comprehensive diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic strategies.

Overall, the interrelationships among chronic kidney disease–related anemia, various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, thalassemia, and related immune‑mediated conditions underscore the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies that address both hematologic abnormalities and their associated systemic manifestations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 241
retrieved_docs.content: (including heart failure, renal failure, retinal disease, metabolic syndrome, anemia, chest and abdominal pain) and common therapeutic agents, underscoring the necessity of integrated assessment and coordinated management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 4 -- Community 2888
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with anemia (فقر الدم), as the sudden loss of renal function often leads to reduced production of erythropoietin and impaired clearance of uremic toxins, which together contribute to a decrease in red blood cell count. This relationship highlights the importance of monitoring hemoglobin levels in patients with acute renal injury and addressing anemia promptly to improve oxygen delivery, reduce cardiovascular strain, and support overall recovery. Effective management of both conditions is essential for optimizing patient outcomes in the setting of acute kidney dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
retrieved_docs.content: disorders caused by inadequate intake of vitamins A, B1, B12 and other micronutrients, and they underlie macrocytic anemia (فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات) that is treated by folic‑acid and vitamin B12 supplementation. Erythropoiesis‑stimulating agents such as darbepoetin alfa are classified among “other antianemic preparations” and complement micronutrient therapy in anemia management. Overall, the knowledge base depicts iron‑containing multivitamins as heterogeneous oral supplements that integrate iron with folic acid, B‑vitamins, vitamin C, essential fatty acids, probiotics, and a broad array of minerals; they are organized hierarchically under multivitamin‑plus‑mineral categories, tailored for specific populations (e.g., bariatric, prenatal), and employed to correct combined micronutrient deficiencies, support hematopoiesis, enhance iron absorption, and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIANEMIC PREPARATIONS are classified as vitamin B12 and folic‑acid supplements; they contain both vitamin B12 and folic acid and are used to treat or prevent anemia that results from deficiencies of these essential nutrients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 49
retrieved_docs.content: anemia (Arabic: فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات), reflecting the well‑established relationship that folate deficiency can cause this anemia. By delivering iron together with folic acid and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, fatty acids, and supportive agents, IRON/MULTIVITAMINS aim to correct iron‑deficiency anemia, prevent or treat folate‑deficiency‑related macrocytic anemia, and address overall nutritional needs, thereby providing a comprehensive micronutrient therapy for patients at risk of hematologic and nutritional disorders.
